resharedPost.text:
I was an H1-B worker for almost 7 years! Few notes as that way of life comes to a staggering end:

(NOTE please don't add to the anxiety of the situation: After the clarification posted by the US gov recently, most of the current folks are unaffected. Both orders in the comments)

1. Transformative experience
I was an H1-B worker for almost 7 years in addition to 2 years of study. The most formative and fun part of my life was spent learning from the best minds in the US whom I still call upon for advice. Gratitude.

2. Social uplifting
My first years' salary was more money than my entire family had seen in multiple years. It elevated my entire family beyond our circumstances, allowed us to afford decent palliative care when my father was diagnosed with cancer and gave me the financial freedom to pursue entrepreneurship.

3. Don't ask everyone to come back to India
Please don't be tone deaf. Folks have their livelihoods, families and friendships to safely settle into a new rhythm. It's a big change. It takes time to settle your life choices. 

4. To the Indians in the US: choose wisely
The writing has been on the wall for quite some time. Please don't be blind. Consider making your choices consistent with your life wishes. You are among the smartest and most valued class of talent our country has. You will always have options.

5. The US gov
Regardless of abuse of H1-B visa program, the US Gov has a right to think about Americans above Indian citizens. They will exercise this right in ways we don't approve of. 'Hope' is not a strategy.

6. Coming back to India
Even then, If you do choose to come back to India as I did 4+ years ago, a few things to note: Today's India is not the one we left decades ago. Massive shift. You CAN live the lifestyle you want now if you are willing to grow a thicker layer of skin. Having said that, lots of things we counted sanctimonious (value of life, reliable law and order, clean air) might still not be within reach.

7. Settling in India after a long time in the US
Don't move all your life altogether. If possible, do it in batches — one spouse comes before etc. Figure out a) What will you do: revenue streams, intellectual fulfillment et al b) Where will you live: allow your livelihood to transition over years. Metros will offer pockets to settle into immediately and then do other things as your network grows.

On aggregate, it feels like India will gain from putting gates on the brain drain to US. As Amitabh Kant said, "America’s loss will be India’s gain". Having said that, it's not a given. 

What do you think? 

If you or your family need a sounding board, feel free to DM! Will love to tell you about my experience moving back.

As a side note, we are building in Jobs tech attempting to re-arrange the incentives in the ecosystem by helping experienced professionals find high-intellectual-potential opportunities.

Having family nearby, being free with no visa restrictions, having help & support to do household tasks and most of all, having roots to a heritage that is your own— that is worth it, for those who weight that more than the infrastructure that USA provides. 

Also, I will be honest - moving to India involves facing the effects of corruption everyday. And a huge loss in the freedom of speech - can’t call politicians what you think out loud. (USA has art galleries that glorify people calling politicians xxxx in many languages)
Having to figure out healthcare and hold your life in your hands every time you decide to fight for road space - those are irritants that stare you in the face as soon as you move back.  

For anyone heading back, I would say find and connect with those who have moved back, including the author of this post, and get tips. It is do-able, with it’s own interesting challenges.

resharedPost.text:
My mother in the eyes of the world is what is called an "average Indian housewife". Obedient and docile to a fault, she was never known for being assertive. She diligently followed all the rules of the household, took care of my grandparents, cooked, took us to our music classes, and watched over our studies. In short, did all the million and one things that fall to the lot of an "average Indian housewife ".

However, when it came to deciding where I would attend college, I witnessed a different side of her. Like a tigress defending her young, she stood up to the entire family and fought for me to attend college in a faraway town in Karnataka. Her intention was not just to make me a good engineer. She believed that I should live life on my terms, explore the world, meet new people, and establish my identity. That one decision changed my life. Her fierce defiance saved me from becoming someone I would not have identified with. After dropping me off at college, she quietly returned to being the "average Indian housewife."

In 2017, my mother once again demonstrated her unwavering determination and fierceness by helping my sister overcome a personal issue. For the next four years, she was a constant source of support for my sister, never leaving her side. Despite being someone who easily got lost while traveling, my mother traveled frequently to be with my sister whenever she needed her. She shielded my sister from any negativity and never once made her feel as though she needed to reconsider her decisions during those four years.
During this time, my mother even surprised us by learning how to convert Word documents to PDFs, forward them, edit documents, and book travel tickets online. Her willingness to adapt and grow to become my sister's greatest support showed us the remarkable woman she is underneath the veneer of an "average Indian housewife".   

Last year, my sister and I decided to pursue higher education, at a very unconventional age. My mother proudly announced to the world that her daughters were rock stars. In Patna, she is now an inspiration and loving mentor to many girls, like my sister and me, and their families. She is the reason why these girls in our neighborhood are now preparing to start their professional careers with confidence. Little did we know that she had this inner strength, this determination, and this foresight all along, hidden from the world. 

In every aspect of my professional journey, more than anything, these learnings from my mother have helped me the most.

Remember, every woman managing a house and making it a home is exceptional. Irrespective of their professional status, each "average Indian housewife" has high-order management and financial skills. Nothing about them is "average". 
I'm sure this story is not unique, and many of you may be thinking about your mothers as you read this. 

PS: This is a picture of her from 2021 when she got a fancy haircut for the 1st time. Doesn't she have the best smile!! ❤

article.title: Hybrid work arrangements are here to stay, Microsoft says

resharedPost.text:
Earlier this week, I had the opportunity of interviewing with CNBC Squawk Box Asia to discuss some of our latest Work Trend Index findings. I’m excited for what’s around the corner with hybrid work and staying close with customers to learn more from how they continue to navigate some of the biggest challenges from the past year. Some major takeaways to think about :

Gen Z, women and underrepresented minorities have been most negatively affected in terms of career advancement, feeling engaged/excited about work, and getting a word in during meetings.

Hybrid work is going to be the new normal. 70% want flexible work to stay, while over 60% want more in-person time and more than 65% of leaders plan to redesign office space for hybrid work.

Hear more from the interview here:  https://lnkd.in/g7n6HA6
#HybridWork #FutureofWork #RemoteWork
